title: Welcome Figure (spuy’elepebŠ)
plaqueText: This welcome figure, called spuy’elepebŠ, represents our people and culture. The upraised arms invite travelers to Tacoma and are anchored by cedar and a rope of lake weed to show connection between all living things. A collaboration between the City of Tacoma, the Puyallup Tribe, and the Tacoma Art Museum, it faces the water to honor the Puyallup’s identity as the ‘People of the Water.’

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: inscription and tribal collaboration details

sourceNotes.name: Prairie Line Trail
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: physical marker and historical context

storyText:
The Welcome Figure, called spuy’elepebŠ, faces the waters of Commencement Bay with outstretched arms to greet visitors. Carved from cedar and anchored with a rope of lake weed, it symbolizes the
Puyallup people’s connection to land and water. Commissioned by the City of Tacoma, the Puyallup Tribe of Indians and the Tacoma Art Museum, this figure reminds us that Tacoma sits on the ancestral
land of the spuy’elepebŠ—the People of the Water. It honors the resilience of the tribe and invites residents and travelers alike to respect Indigenous culture and history.

title: People of the Water
plaqueText: For generations, Coast Salish people relied on the bay for trade and travel. The People of the Water (spuy’alepabš) were connected to the land and sea, living by fishing, clamming, berry picking and trading. Early Tacomans who came from many nations also used these waterways to seek better lives, and today these waters still nourish our community.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text referencing Coast Salish and Puyallup people

sourceNotes.name: Puyallup Tribe of Indians
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: existence of marker and its cultural context

storyText:
This marker celebrates the Coast Salish people, especially the Puyallup Tribe, whose lives were shaped by the bay. Generations of spuy’alepabš lived here by fishing, clamming and trading along the
waterways. Later immigrants from Asia, Europe and elsewhere followed the same paths seeking better lives. Tacoma’s identity remains tied to these waters, which continue to support local tribes and
communities today.

title: Margaret Meeker Davis
plaqueText: Margaret Meeker Davis (1881–1960) was a Puyallup Indian who raised 14 children here at Old Tacoma. She rowed across Commencement Bay to dig clams and harvest shellfish to support her family. Her life represents the strength of Puyallup women and their care for their people and land.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text and location for Margaret Meeker Davis marker

sourceNotes.name: Puyallup Tribe historical notes (link not working-can't find a substitute for this)
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: context on Margaret’s and her family's life from her daughter Lena Landry Davis

storyText:
Margaret Meeker Davis, a Puyallup Indian woman, raised fourteen children while living near Old Tacoma. To feed her family she rowed across the bay to dig clams and gather shellfish. Her strength and
determination illustrate the role of Puyallup women in sustaining their families despite colonial disruptions. The story of Margaret and her descendants is part of the larger narrative of Indigenous
resilience along Commencement Bay.

title: Nettie Asberry
plaqueText: Nettie J. Asberry (1865–1968) earned a doctorate in music and became a pioneering civil rights leader. She organized the Clover Leaf Art Club and founded the Tacoma chapter of the NAACP in 1913. Her home here served as a cultural and political center for Tacoma’s Black community.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text about Nettie Asberry and her achievements

sourceNotes.name: National Park Service - Nettie Craig Asberry
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: historical context of Asberry

storyText:
Born in Kansas in 1865, Nettie J. Asberry earned a doctorate in music and moved to Tacoma in 1888. She founded the Clover Leaf Art Club and helped establish the Tacoma branch of the NAACP in 1913, one
of the first on the West Coast. Her home on South 13th Street became a gathering place for artists and civil rights activists. Asberry’s life stands at the intersection of arts and social justice,
reminding us of the leadership of Black women in Tacoma’s history.

title: Browns Point Light Station — Puyallup Homeland
plaqueText: For thousands of years, this area has been the traditional homeland of the Puyallup people. Browns Point was part of the Puyallup Tribal Reservation and was deeded to tribal members Joe Douette and Jerry Meeker. Despite later federal appropriation of the land for a lighthouse, the site’s Indigenous heritage endures. This marker honors the Puyallup as original stewards of the Point and acknowledges the loss of their land.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: inscription referencing Puyallup homeland and Brown’s Point history

sourceNotes.name: US Lighthouse Society archives
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: history of Browns Point and Puyallup land transfer

storyText:
Long before the federal government built a lighthouse at Browns Point, this headland belonged to the Puyallup people. In the late 19th century the land was deeded to tribal members Joe Douette and
Jerry Meeker. The U.S. Lighthouse Service later appropriated the site for navigational purposes, erecting a light that guided ships into the bay. Today’s marker acknowledges the site’s tribal origin,
the loss of Indigenous land, and the ongoing presence of the Puyallup Tribe in the region.

title: Our First Settlers — S’Puyalupubsh Camp
plaqueText: At this site on Commencement Bay, S’Puyalupubsh Indian fishermen camped to fish, dig clams and rest. Their visits gave this area the name ‘Tacoma’, meaning ‘Mother of Waters’. By 1894 hotels and boathouses thrived here, but this plaque remembers the Puyallup people who were here long before settlers arrived.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text about S’Puyalupubsh camp and origin of the name Tacoma

sourceNotes.name: History Link.org
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: history of Tacoma and history of Indigenous camps

storyText:
Before Tacoma became a city, S’Puyalupubsh fishermen set up seasonal camps along this bay to fish, dig clams and gather shellfish. They called the area 'Tacoma,' meaning Mother of Waters. As settlers
arrived and the shoreline filled with hotels and boathouses, Indigenous camps were erased. This marker reminds us that the city’s name and prosperity are rooted in the Puyallup people’s relationship
to these waters.

title: Commemorating Nihongo Gakko (Japanese Language School)
plaqueText: Opened in 1922, the Tacoma Japanese Language School (Nihongo Gakko) served as the heart of Tacoma’s Japanese American community. Children attended after public school to learn Japanese language and culture. After the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor, the school was used to register Tacoma’s Japanese residents for forced removal. Though the community reopened the school after World War II, the building was later razed. This marker honors those who gathered here to learn, teach, and endure.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: inscription about the Japanese Language School and forced registration during WWII

sourceNotes.name: University of Washington, Tacoma Japanese Language School Memorial
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: historical context of the Japanese Language School

storyText:
The Tacoma Japanese Language School, or Nihongo Gakko, opened in 1922 to serve the growing Japanese American community. Students gathered after public school to study Japanese and maintain cultural
traditions. After the Pearl Harbor attack in 1941, federal authorities used the school to register Tacoma’s Japanese residents for forced removal to incarceration camps. Although the community
reopened the school after World War II, the building was eventually demolished. This marker honors the Japanese American families who learned, taught and endured here.

title: Chinese Expulsion: Aftermath and Indictments
plaqueText: In 1885, U.S. Attorney W.H. White wired that the evidence warranted arrests after Tacoma citizens forcibly expelled hundreds of Chinese residents on November 3. A grand jury indicted 27 civic leaders for rioting, arson and unlawful assembly, but charges were dismissed or never enforced. No one was ever held responsible. Chinese did not return to Tacoma until the 1920s.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: full plaque text about indictments after the Chinese expulsion and coordinates

sourceNotes.name: Tacoma Chinese Reconciliation Project Foundation
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: background on the expulsion and reconciliation

storyText:
On November 3, 1885, more than 500 residents forcibly marched Tacoma’s Chinese community to a train station and burned Chinatown. U.S. Attorney W.H. White wired that the evidence warranted arrests. A
grand jury indicted 27 civic leaders for rioting and arson, but the cases were dismissed or never pursued. This marker highlights the failure of justice and notes that Chinese families did not return
to Tacoma until decades later.

title: Chinese Expulsion: Climate of Exclusion
plaqueText: Reverend W.D. McFarland preached in Tacoma’s churches that Chinese immigration was a curse and warned that “hordes of Chinamen” threatened American jobs. Congress responded with the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882. Tacoma’s leaders echoed the cry “The Chinese Must Go,” creating a climate that set the stage for the expulsion.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text describing anti-Chinese sentiment and the Exclusion Act

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink.org article on Chinese expulsion
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: context for sermons and exclusion laws

storyText:
Decades of anti-Chinese agitation preceded the 1885 expulsion. Preachers like Reverend W.D. McFarland declared Chinese immigration a curse and warned congregations that “hordes of Chinamen” threatened
white livelihoods. Congress responded with the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882. Tacoma’s leaders echoed the cry “The Chinese Must Go,” creating a climate that normalized violence. This marker documents
the rhetoric that made the expulsion possible.

title: Chinese Expulsion: Planning of the Expulsion
plaqueText: On October 31, 1885, Tacoma Mayor Jacob Weisback and a committee of business leaders gave the city’s Chinese residents three days to leave. On November 3 a mob of more than 500 forced families at gunpoint onto wagons and marched them to the Lake View depot. Their homes and businesses were burned. This coordinated violence became known as the “Tacoma Method.”

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text summarizing the planning and expulsion actions of 1885

sourceNotes.name: The Tacoma Method
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: additional context on the “Tacoma Method” and expulsion

storyText:
Tacoma’s Chinese expulsion was carefully planned. On October 31, 1885, Mayor Jacob Weisback and a committee of prominent citizens gave the Chinese residents three days to leave. When the deadline
passed, a mob rounded up families at gunpoint, loaded them onto wagons and marched them to the Lake View depot. Their homes and businesses were torched. The so‑called “Tacoma Method” became a model
for similar actions across the West.

title: Journey to Gold Mountain
plaqueText: Chinese immigrants set sail for “Gold Mountain” seeking opportunity and escape from poverty and persecution. The journey across the Pacific was perilous; thousands died. Those who survived faced discrimination but forged tight-knit communities. Tacoma’s early Chinese community, once centered near this waterfront, contributed to the city’s development until the expulsion of 1885.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text about Chinese immigrants’ journey and hardships

sourceNotes.name: Chinese Reconciliation Project Foundation
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: additional background on Gold Mountain migrants

storyText:
During the 19th century, thousands of Chinese men and women embarked on perilous voyages to “Gold Mountain” (America). Many sought fortune in the gold fields or on the railroads but faced
discrimination and violence upon arrival. Those who settled in Tacoma built businesses along the waterfront. This vibrant community ended abruptly when city leaders expelled them in 1885. The marker
honors their journey and contributions.

title: Welcome to the Tacoma Chinese Reconciliation Park
plaqueText: The Tacoma Chinese Reconciliation Park honors the Chinese community expelled from Tacoma in 1885 and celebrates the city’s commitment to healing and diversity. Visitors can walk interpretive paths telling the story of the expulsion, the community’s resilience, and the ongoing work of the Chinese Reconciliation Project Foundation. Please enjoy this place of beauty, remembrance and reconciliation.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text welcoming visitors to the park

sourceNotes.name: Chinese Reconciliation Park Foundation
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: background on the park and interpretive program

storyText:
The Tacoma Chinese Reconciliation Park stands on the site where Chinese homes once stood. Created by the Chinese Reconciliation Project Foundation and the City of Tacoma, the park invites visitors to
walk interpretive pathways that tell the story of the 1885 expulsion, acknowledge the harm done, and celebrate the resilience of Chinese Americans. This marker welcomes you to a space dedicated to
healing and cultural understanding.

title: Journey to Reconciliation
plaqueText: Imagine being forced to march from your home, escorted by armed men, while a crowd cheers. Governor Gary Locke asked us to place ourselves in the shoes of the expelled Chinese. The Chinese Reconciliation Project Foundation was formed to heal this wound through education, art and dialogue. Tacoma acknowledges past wrongs and commits to a future where everyone belongs.

sourceNotes.name: Historical Marker Database
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: plaque text quoting Governor Locke and explaining reconciliation efforts

sourceNotes.name: Washington State history resources
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: context on the expulsion and reconciliation process

storyText:
Governor Gary Locke asked us to imagine the fear and pain of Tacoma’s Chinese residents as they were forced to march to the train station in 1885. In response, the Chinese Reconciliation Project
Foundation formed to heal that trauma through education, art and dialogue. This marker reminds us that reconciliation is an ongoing journey—one that requires acknowledging past injustice and
committing to a future where people of all backgrounds belong.

title: Dr. Maxine Mimms
plaqueText: Dr. Maxine Mimms (1928-2024) founded Tacoma Evergreen, the only community owned Black college in the Pacific Northwest.

sourceNotes.name: Evergreen
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Dr. Maxine Mimms' legacy as a founder and the campus history

sourceNotes.name: Dr. Maxine Mimms Presents
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Biographical deatils,

storyText: Dr. Maxine Buie Mimms was a master architect of urban education who famously refused to use the word 'retirement,' preferring to call it 'reassignment.' Her journey began in Newport News, Virginia, fueled by her father’s Marcus Garvey-inspired philosophy of self-actualization. After becoming Seattle’s first contracted Black elementary teacher, she joined the faculty of Evergreen State College in 1972. Seeing the barriers facing Hilltop residents, she began holding classes at her kitchen table at 5:00 AM to accommodate working adults. This grassroots initiative blossomed into the Evergreen Tacoma campus. Dr. Mimms’ vision was rooted in the 'Sankofa' principle—reaching back to gather knowledge from the past to build a better future—transforming the Hilltop into a global model for community-centered higher education.

title: The Evergreen State College – Tacoma Campus
plaqueText: Emerging from community struggles for access to higher education in the 1970s and '80, Evergreen-Tacoma is the only community-owned Black college in the Pacific Northwest

sourceNotes.name: Evergreen
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: establishment on Evergreen College - Tacoma

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink - Evergreen State College
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Origin of establishment and legacy

storyText: Evergreen-Tacoma represents a hard-won victory for educational equity. In the early 1970s, the Hilltop community was an 'educational desert,' lacking a four-year public institution. While the main campus in Olympia felt worlds away for working-class residents, local activists led by Dr. Maxine Mimms and Dr. Elizabeth Duncan Koontz fought to bring the classroom to the neighborhood. By 1982, the program transitioned from kitchen tables and storefronts into a formally accredited branch of the state college. Unlike traditional campuses, it was designed with the working adult in mind, featuring a 'bridge' curriculum and a culture rooted in the African proverb 'It takes a whole village to raise a child.' In 2001, the campus moved into its permanent home on 6th Avenue, standing as a monument to the community’s belief that local knowledge and academic excellence are inseparable.

title: E.S. Brazil
plaqueText: Elmer 'E.S' Brazil was a pioneering Tacoma businessman, civic activist, and a force for the economic opportunity in the Black community during the mid-20th century.

title: Shiloh Baptist Church
plaqueText: Shiloh Baptist Church, established in 1953, quickly became a spiritual and civic home for Black families in Tacoma.

sourceNotes.name: Spark Northwest
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Church history and Oasis of Hope

sourceNotes.name: African American Studies Program
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: historical significance of the Shiloh Baptist Church

storyText: Founded in 1953, Shiloh Baptist Church quickly outgrew its humble beginnings, evolving into a central hub for social justice and community resilience. Under the long-standing leadership of Pastor Gregory Christopher and the late Pastor Dr. E.S. Brazill, the church has balanced spiritual guidance with radical community care. In 1999, it expanded its mission by establishing the Oasis of Hope Center, which provides youth mentoring, educational programs, and low-income housing. Today, Shiloh remains at the forefront of Tacoma’s progress, championing environmental sustainability through solar power initiatives and continuing its legacy as a sanctuary for both the soul and the neighborhood.

title: USO Club #2
plaqueText: The United Service Organizations (USO) club #2 was established in 1942 to serve families during a time when the military was still segregated.

sourceNotes.name: Social Welfare History Project
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Historical context of United Service Organizations

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink - USO clubs in Tacoma
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: USO establishment in Tacoma

storyText: Dedicated on September 4, 1942, USO Club #2 was more than just a recreational center; it was a 'home away from home' for nearly a million Black service members and war workers who were barred from Tacoma's white-only venues. One of its most legendary moments occurred in 1943 when Navy Cross recipient and Pearl Harbor hero Doris 'Dorie' Miller visited the club. Despite the era’s segregation, the club became a vibrant hub of jazz and community, hosting integrated musical events that challenged the racial boundaries of the time.

title: Pythian Temple
plaqueText: The Pythian Temple was built in 1906 by the Knights of Pythias fraternal organization and was designed by local architect, Frederick Heath in the Beaux-Arts style.

sourceNotes.name: We R Tacoma
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Frederick Heath and his involvement in designing the Pythian Temple

storyText: Designed by the same architect behind Stadium High School, the Pythian Temple is a 'hidden gem' of the Theater District. While the street-level facade is modest sandstone, the interior houses the magnificent Castle Hall—a soaring, two-story ceremonial chamber that remains virtually untouched since 1907. Complete with original Douglas fir paneling, ornate murals depicting the Greek legend of Damon and Pythias, and three massive art-glass skylights, the building serves as a rare time capsule of fraternal life in the early 20th century. Today, it continues to serve the Knights of Pythias while also hosting the Tacoma Youth Theater and local arts groups.

title: Elks Temple
plaqueText: The Elks Temple is a historic Beaux Arts Fraternal building built in 1916 for the Fraternal Order of Elks. It now houses the McMenamins Elks Temple hotel, restaurant and event space.

sourceNotes.name: South Sound - McMenamins Elks Temple
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Elks Temple during and after the Elks vacated the building

sourceNotes.name: Washington Trust for Historic Preservation
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Restoration efforts to save Elks

storyText: The Elks Temple was once the epicenter of Tacoma’s social life, featuring a grand ballroom and a sprawling indoor pool. For decades, it stood as a symbol of the city's early 20th-century prosperity. However, as fraternal memberships declined, the building was vacated in the 1960s and fell into deep disrepair, becoming a haunt for urban explorers and graffiti artists. After sitting derelict for over 40 years, it underwent a massive restoration by McMenamins and reopened in 2019. Today, the building retains its original ornate plasterwork and Spanish-tiled roof, blending its stately Beaux-Arts past with modern Pacific Northwest culture.

title: Point Defiance Park
plaqueText: Point Defiance Park the northern tip of Tocoma, where a 19th century military reservation once stood before becoming one of the largest and oldest urban parks in the Pacific Northwest.

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink - Point Defiance Park (Tacoma)
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: The 1841 Wilkes Expedition and the 1888 transition from military reserve to park.

sourceNotes.name: Metro Parks Tacoma - Point Defiance History
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Presidential acts by Cleveland and Roosevelt granting land title to the city.

storyText: The park owes its name to Captain Charles Wilkes, who in 1841 noted that the high bluffs could 'bid defiance to any attack' if fortified. While President Andrew Johnson set the land aside as a military reserve in 1866, no cannons were ever installed. Instead, Tacoma’s civic leaders lobbied Congress to transform the wild peninsula into a public refuge. In 1888, President Grover Cleveland authorized its use as a park, and by 1905, President Theodore Roosevelt signed the land over to the city entirely. Today, it remains a 760-acre sanctuary of old-growth forest, historic gardens, and the renowned Point Defiance Zoo & Aquarium.

title: Division Street
plaqueText: In the 20th century, many African American families settled here, especially after World War II, when they came to work in the shipyeards and military bases.

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Redlining, Racial Covenants, and Housing Discrimination in Tacoma

sourceNotes.name: Racial Restictive Covenants Project
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Historical segregation and the geographical boundaries in Tacoma

storyText: During World War II, Tacoma’s population surged as thousands of African Americans migrated from the South to work at the Seattle-Tacoma Shipbuilding Corporation and nearby Fort Lewis. Despite facing systemic housing discrimination and 'Jim Crow' conditions in the shipyards, Black families established a vibrant community in the Hilltop neighborhood, centered around Division Street. These newcomers transformed Tacoma’s social and cultural landscape, laying the groundwork for the city's future civil rights leadership.

title: George Putnam Riley
plaqueText: George Putnam Riley (1833-1905) was a Black abolitionist, writer, and political leader who migrated to the Pacific Northwest after the Civil War.

sourceNotes.name: Oregon Encyclopedia
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: biographical details of George P. Riley

sourceNotes.name: Seattle pi
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: overview of George P. Riley's accomplishments in Tacoma

storyText: George Putnam Riley was a formidable orator and a pioneer of Black civil rights in the West. After moving to Tacoma, he became a central figure in the city's early political life, co-founding the Workingmen's Joint Stock Association and advocating for enfranchisement. His legacy serves as a reminder of the influential Black leaders who helped build the civic foundations of the Pacific Northwest.

title: Joye Hardiman Park
plaqueText: Named for Dr. Joye Hardiman, a longtime director of Evergreen-Tacoma. This site honors her pioneering contributions to education and community empowerment.

sourceNotes.name: News Tribune
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Who Joye Haridiman is and her significance

sourceNotes.name: Evergreen
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Biographical details

title: Horace Lawhorn's House
plaqueText: Horace Lawhorn was a longshoreman, labor activist, and respected community leader who fought for racial equity on Tacoma's docks and in its labor unions.

sourceNotes.name: Black Historic Sites WA
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Significance of Horace Lawhorn

sourceNotes.name: DAHP
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: brief context of Horace Lawhorn's occupation

storyText: Arriving in 1890, Horace Lawhorn initially worked as a police wagon driver before becoming the city's first Black police officer in 1899, a role that led the department to adopt a permanent policy of racial inclusion. Beyond the badge, Lawhorn was a key figure in the labor movement and real estate, physically constructing his family home in the Hilltop neighborhood.

title: Caballeros Club
plaqueText: Founded in 1957, the Caballeros Club was one of Tacoma's earliest Black-owned social organizations. For decades, the Club offered a safe and welcoming space for Black residents to gather.

sourceNotes.name: News Tribune
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Date of establishment and first hand accounts of community

sourceNotes.name: Black Historic Sites WA
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: Status as Tacoma's oldest privately owned Black social club and Ray Gibson's legacy

storyText: At a time when many public and private venues in Tacoma remained informally segregated, the Caballeros Club emerged as a premier destination for Black social life.

title: Dr. Nettie Craig Asberry's House
plaqueText: Dr. Nettie Craig Asberry (1865-1968) was a classically trained musician, educator, and one of Tacoma's most influential civil rights leaders.

sourceNotes.name: HistoryLink - Asberry, Nettie Craig
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: verification of Dr. Nettie Asberry's involvement during the civil rights era and early years

sourceNotes.name: National Park Service - Nettie Craig Asberry
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: more context on Dr. Nettie Asberry's life and founding of Tacoma's NAACP

storyText: In 1913, Dr. Nettie Asberry founded the Tacoma NAACP, one of the earliest branches in the nation, and hosted organizing meetings in her home. She championed women's suffrage, fought discrimination in schools, and nurtured the city's Black cultural life through her piano instruction and leadership in civic groups. Her Hiltop residence remains the cornerstone of Tacoma's civil right legacy.

title: Bethlehem Baptist Church
plaqueText: Founded in 1890, Bethlehem Baptist Church is Tacoma's oldest Black Baptist congregation and one of the city's earliest Black institutions.

sourceNotes.name: Bethlehem Baptist Church
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: context of origin and date of establishment

sourceNotes.name: Tacoma Public Library History
sourceNotes.whatItVerifies: early congregational record

storyText: Originally gathering in the home of its founders just one year after Washington achieved statehood, Bethlehem Baptist Church served as a vital anchor for Black families arriving in the Pacific Northwest. Under the early leadership of Rev. J.B. Beckly, the church quickly grew from a small prayer circle into a spiritual and social headquarters.
